coredata.dc:title: Dietary Plukenetia conophora seed alters muscle cholesterol, antioxidant enzymes, and fatty acids in growing rabbits
coredata.dc:creator: Adeyemi, Kazeem D.
Oladele, Taiwo O.
Atolani, Olubunmi
Sulaimon, Rasheed O.
Zubair, Jamiu I.
Ajao, Babatunde H.
coredata.dc:description: The influence of dietary inclusion of Plukenetia conophora seed (PCS) on growth, carcass, muscle antioxidant enzymes, fatty acids, meat quality, and sensory attributes of Longissimus thoracis et lumburum muscle in rabbits was examined. Seventy-two, 28 d old male New Zealand rabbits (750 ± 20 g) were randomly allotted to diets containing either no PCS (PCS-0), 2.5% PCS (PCS-2.5) or 5% PCS (PCS-5) for eight weeks, and euthanized. PCS-5 rabbits had higher (P < 0.05) body and carcass weights than the PCS-0 rabbits. Dietary PCS improved feed efficiency in rabbits. Muscle antioxidant enzymes activities and total phenols were higher while muscle cholesterol was lower (P < 0.05) in supplemented meat than the PCS-0 meat. The concentration of C22:6n-3, C20:5n-3 and C18:3n-3 was higher (P < 0.05) in the supplemented meat than the PCS-0 meat. Sensory attributes, carbonyl, and TBARS values and physicochemical properties of meat did not differ among diets. Supplementation of PCS-5 enhances muscle n-3 fatty acids without impairing the sensory properties, and oxidative stability of rabbit meat.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carcass
Growth
Longissimus
Phenol
Sensory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Meat quality,oxidative stability, and antioxidant enzymes
Lipid extraction and fatty acid analysis
Chemical composition, cholesterol, and total phenol content of rabbit meat

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ingredients, chemical and fatty acid composition of dietary treatments and Plukenetia conophora seed.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: PCS-0, Diet without P. conophora seed (PCS); PCS-2.5, Diet containing 2.5% PCS; PCS-5, Diet containing 5% PCS.

Supplied per kg diet: thiamine 1.43 mg; α-tocopherol 26.8 mg; Retinol 3.45 mg; riboflavin 3.44 mg; biotin 0.05 mg; pantothenic acid 6.46 mg; Niacin 40.17 mg; folic acid 0.56 mg; cholicalciferol 43.8 μg; pyridoxine 2.29 mg; cyanocobalamin 0.05 mg; menadione 2.29 mg; Iron 120 mg; Zinc 120 mg; Copper 15 mg; Cobalt 0.4 mg; Selenium 0.3 mg; Manganese 150 mg; Iodine 1.5 mg.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Growth indices in rabbits⁎ fed diet supplemented with Plukenetia conophora seeds.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a,b,c, Means with different superscripts in a row differ significantly (P < 0.05).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Eight replicates per dietary group; three rabbits per replicate.
PCS-0, Diet without P. conophora seed (PCS); PCS-2.5, Diet containing 2.5% PCS; PCS-5, Diet containing 5% PCS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carcass traits of rabbits⁎ fed diets supplemented with Plukenetia conophora seeds.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a,b Means within a row with different superscripts differ significantly (P < 0.05).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Eight replicates per dietary group; three rabbits per replicate.
PCS-0, Diet without P. conophora seed (PCS); PCS-2.5, Diet containing 2.5% PCS; PCS-5, Diet containing 5% PCS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical composition, cholesterol, and phenol content of Longissimus thoracis et lumburum muscle in rabbits⁎ supplemented with Plukenetia conophora seeds

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a,b,c Means within a row with different superscripts differ significantly (P < 0.05).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Eight replicates per dietary group; three rabbits per replicate.
PCS-0, Diet without P. conophora seed (PCS); PCS-2.5, Diet containing 2.5% PCS; PCS-5, Diet containing 5% PCS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Antioxidant enzymes of Longissimus thoracis et lumburum in rabbits⁎ supplemented with Plukenetia conophora seeds.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a,b,c, means with different superscripts in a row differ significantly (P < 0.05).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Eight replicates per dietary group; three rabbits per replicate.
PCS-0, Diet without P. conophora seed (PCS); PCS-2.5, Diet containing 2.5% PCS; PCS-5, Diet containing 5% PCS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physicochemical properties of Longissimus thoracis et lumburum muscle in rabbits⁎ supplemented with Plukenetia conophora seed.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a,b,c, means with different superscripts within a column differ significantly (P < 0.05).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Eight replicates per dietary group; three rabbits per replicate.
PCS-0, Diet without Plukenetia Conophora seed (PCS); PCS-2.5, Diet containing 2.5% PCS; PCS-5, Diet containing 5% PCS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fatty acid profile (mg/100 g fresh tissue) of Longissimus thoracis et lumburum in rabbits⁎ supplemented with Plukenetia conophora seeds.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a,b,c means with different superscripts along the same row differ significantly (P < 0.05). SEM, standard error of mean.1PCS-0, Diet without P. conophora seed (PCS); PCS-2.5, Diet containing 2.5% PCS; PCS-5, Diet containing 5% PCS.SFA, saturated fatty acid; MUFA, monounsaturated fatty acid; PUFA, polyunsaturated fatty acids. ΣSFA = (C12:0 + C14:0 + C15:0 + C16:0 + C17:0 + C18:0), ΣMUFA = (C14:1n-5 + C16:1–7 + C18:1n-9 + C20:1n-9), Σn-3 = (C18:3n-3 + C20:5n-3 + C22:5n-3 + C20:3n-3 + C22:6n-3), Σn-6 = (C18:2n-6 + C20:3n-6 + C20:4n-6), n-6:n-3 = (C18:2n-6 + C20:3n-6 + C20:4n-6), ΣPUFA + (Σn-3 + Σn-6).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Eight replicates per dietary group; three rabbits per replicate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sensory profile of Longissimus thoracis et lumburum muscle in rabbits supplemented with Plukenetia conophora seeds.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: PCS-0, Diet without P. conophora seed (PCS); PCS-2.5, Diet containing 2.5% PCS; PCS-5, Diet containing 5% PCS. SEM, standard error of mean.

head.ce:title: Dietary Plukenetia conophora seed alters muscle cholesterol, antioxidant enzymes, and fatty acids in growing rabbits

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Animal Production, Faculty of Agriculture, University of Ilorin, PMB 1515 Ilorin, Kwara State, Nigeria

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Animal Production, Faculty of Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Animal Production, Faculty of Agriculture, University of Ilorin, PMB 1515, Ilorin, Kwara State, Nigeria.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Physical Sciences, University of Ilorin, PMB 1515 Ilorin, Kwara State, Nigeria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Animal Nutrition, College of Animal Science, Federal University of Agriculture, PMB 2373 Markurdi, Benue State, Nigeria
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Animal Nutrition, College of Animal Science, Federal University of Agriculture, PMB 2373, Markurdi, Benue State, Nigeria..

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The influence of dietary inclusion of Plukenetia conophora seed (PCS) on growth, carcass, muscle antioxidant enzymes, fatty acids, meat quality, and sensory attributes of Longissimus thoracis et lumburum muscle in rabbits was examined. Seventy-two, 28 d old male New Zealand rabbits (750 ± 20 g) were randomly allotted to diets containing either no PCS (PCS-0), 2.5% PCS (PCS-2.5) or 5% PCS (PCS-5) for eight weeks, and euthanized. PCS-5 rabbits had higher (P < 0.05) body and carcass weights than the PCS-0 rabbits. Dietary PCS improved feed efficiency in rabbits. Muscle antioxidant enzymes activities and total phenols were higher while muscle cholesterol was lower (P < 0.05) in supplemented meat than the PCS-0 meat. The concentration of C22:6n-3, C20:5n-3 and C18:3n-3 was higher (P < 0.05) in the supplemented meat than the PCS-0 meat. Sensory attributes, carbonyl, and TBARS values and physicochemical properties of meat did not differ among diets. Supplementation of PCS-5 enhances muscle n-3 fatty acids without impairing the sensory properties, and oxidative stability of rabbit meat.

ce:section.ce:para: There is rapidly growing and varying consumer preference for healthy, sustainable, and high quality meat and meat products (Teixeira & Rodrigues, 2021). To meet the diversifying consumer demands, the livestock industry must ensure that robust and innovative livestock production strategies are developed in order to consistently produce safe, nutritious, and healthy meat and meat products (Olynk, 2012; Widmar & Ortega, 2014).

The global rabbit meat production is estimated at 1.8 million ton, which comprise of rabbit meat from Asia (75.3%), Europe (21.3%), Africa (7.1%) and the Americas (1.2%) (FAOSTAT, 2020). In Africa, the production of rabbit meat increased from 15,091 ton in 1971 to 99,933 ton in 2020, which depicts an annual average growth rate of 4.10% (FAOSTAT, 2020), and suggests that rabbit meat has the potential to alleviate food insecurity in the region (FAO, 2019).

Rabbit meat is valuable in many cultural culinary traditions (Abdel-Naeem, Sallam, & Zaki, 2021; Dalle Zotte et al., 2020), and it is rich in monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA) (Dal Bosco et al., 2018; Mattioli et al., 2020; Peiretti, Gasco, Brugiapaglia, & Gai, 2011; Perna, Simonetti, Grassi, & Gambacorta, 2019). However, the n-6/n-3 ratio of rabbit meat is >4 (Peiretti & Meineri, 2008; Perna et al., 2019). Elevated n-6/n-3 ratio if replicated in the entire diet could impair the health of consumers (Gebauer, Harris, Kris-Etherton, & Etherton, 2019; Stanley et al., 2007). As a result, there is a growing interest in the use of oil seeds that are rich in n-3 fatty acids (FA), such as linseed (Dal Bosco et al., 2018; Mattioli et al., 2020), chia seed (Peiretti & Meineri, 2008), and perilla seed (Peiretti et al., 2011) to enrich the n-3 PUFA contents of rabbit meat. Nonetheless, most of the aforementioned oil seeds are not readily available in some countries and thus their incorporation into livestock diet may be cost-prohibitive. This heightens the need to explore other oil seeds that could be utilized to enrich the n-3 PUFA of rabbit meat. One of the promising oil seeds is Plukenetia conophora (African walnut) (Kanu, Kalu, & Okorie, 2015). P. conophora seed (PCS) contains about 48–55% oil on a dry matter basis (Nkwonta, Alamar, Landahl, & Terry, 2016; Uhunmwangho & Omoregie, 2017). PCS oil contains about 64% α-Linolenic acid,11% oleic acid and 15% palmitic and stearic acids (Adeyemi et al., 2020; Kanu et al., 2015). A previous study reported that PCS inclusion ameliorated the n-3 PUFA content of broiler meat (Adeyemi et al., 2020). However, no information is available on the possibility of ameliorating the n-3 PUFA content of rabbit meat via dietary PCS. Owing to the FA composition of PCS, it was hypothesized that the dietary inclusion of PCS would enrich the n-3 PUFA content of rabbit meat.

The high susceptibility of n-3 FA to oxidative deterioration (Miyashita, 2014) forms the major barrier towards the shelf life, safety, and oxidative stability of animal products enriched with n-3 PUFA (Adeyemi et al., 2020; Mattioli et al., 2020). For these reasons, incorporation of antioxidants becomes necessary. PCS is rich in myriad phytochemicals, with potent antioxidant properties (Ademiluyi et al., 2015; Udedi et al., 2014). Thus, we propose that dietary incorporation of PCS in rabbit diets would attenuate oxidative spoilage of n-3 PUFA enriched rabbit meat. Production system and diet can affect the sensory quality of rabbit meat (Castrica et al., 2020; Dal Bosco, Castellini, Bianchi, & Mugnai, 2004; Dalle Zotte, 2002; Volek, Bureš, & Uhlířová, 2018). Thus, it is rational to propose that alterations in animal diet or production system could affect consumer acceptability of rabbit meat. The objective of this study was to determine the influence of dietary inclusion of PCS on growth performance, carcass attributes, fatty acid, cholesterol content, antioxidant status, meat quality, and sensory properties of Longissimus thoracis et lumborum muscle in rabbits.
The experiment was carried out according to the protocol approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee, University of Ilorin (FERC/ASN/2020/111).
Plukenetia Conophora were purchased from a market in Esa-Oke, Osun State, Nigeria. The nuts were deshelled, and cut into pieces, air-dried, and put in an airtight container until usage.

Seventy-two 28 d old male New Zealand rabbits (BW, 750 ± 20 g) were utilized for the feeding trial. After arrival, the rabbits were weighed, and randomly distributed into 24 wooden pens, which had feeders and drinkers. The pens were randomly allotted to either PCS-0, basal diet (BD) only; PCS-2.5, BD + 2.5% PCS; or PCS-5, BD + 5% PCS, for 56 d. Each dietary group had eight replicates with three rabbits per replicate. The diets were formulated according to the National Research Council requirements for rabbits (NRC, 1977). The PCS and other ingredients were milled, homogenized, and pelleted. Feed was prepared weekly. The chemical composition of PCS and dietary treatments was determined in accordance to the methods of AOAC (AOAC, 1990). The phytochemical analysis of PCS and dietary treatments was determined as outlined by Ademiluyi et al. (2015) and Yadav and Agarwala (2011). Rabbits were allowed ad libitum access to feed and water during the feeding trial. The ingredients, chemical, phytochemical, and FA composition of diets and PCS are presented in Table 1 .
Body weight (BW) and feed intake (FI) per pen were recorded weekly. Body weight gain (BWG) and feed conversion ratio (FCR) were calculated.

On the last day of the feeding trial, rabbits were fasted overnight with unrestricted access to water. Thereafter, all the rabbits (eight replicates/dietary group, three rabbits/replicate) were euthanized, flayed, and eviscerated. Carcasses were sectioned, and evaluated following the recommendations of the World Rabbit Science Association (Blasco & Ouhayoun, 1996). Within 14–23 min postmortem, eviscerated carcasses were weighed (Hot carcass weight, HCW). Thereafter, the carcasses were chilled (4 °C) for 24 h and reweighed (Cold carcass weight, CCW). Carcass by-products (skin, head, feet, tail, and dissectible fats) and internal organs (heart, liver, kidney, lungs with trachea, spleen, stomach, small intestine, and caecum) were weighed, and their proportions were calculated as a percentage of slaughter weight (SW). The carcasses were sectioned into fore part (from the atlas vertebra to the 12th thoracic vertebra), intermediate part (from the 12th thoracic vertebra to the 7th lumbar vertebra) and hind part (from the 7th lumbar vertebra) and weighed and their proportions were calculated as a percentage of CCW. Dressing % was calculated as:
Dressing % =
CCW SW
x
100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Meat quality,oxidative stability, and antioxidant enzymes
ce:section.ce:para: Meat quality characteristics were measured on Longissimus thoracis et lumburum (LTL, from the 1st to the 7th lumbar vertebrae) muscle that were excised from both sides of the rabbits (eight replicates/dietary group; three rabbits/replicate). Before analysis, skin, external fat, and epimysium connective tissue were removed from samples. Total phenol content of LTL was assessed using Folin–Ciocalteu method as detailed by Adeyemi (2021). Chemical composition of LTL samples was determined by the AOAC (1990) method. Color coordinates, drip loss, pH, and cooking loss were assessed as described by Adeyemi (2021). Catalase (CAT), Glutathione peroxidase (GPx), and superoxide dismutase (SOD), protein carbonyls, and malondialdehyde (MDA) were assessed with ELISA kits (MyBiosource, San Diego, CA, USA) according to the manufacturer's procedures. Meat cholesterol was quantified as described by Sale´, Marchesini, Fishman, and Berra (1984).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lipid extraction and fatty acid analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Total lipids from pulverized right LTL muscle (eight replicates/dietary group; three rabbits/replicate) and feed samples (three samples per dietary group) were extracted using chloroform: methanol (2:1 v/v) as described by Folch, Lees, and Sloane-Stanley (1957). To obtain the fatty acid methyl esters (FAME), the extracted lipids were treated with 0.66 N KOH in methanol and 14% methanolic BF3 according to the methods of AOAC (1990). Heneicosanoic acid (Sigma-Aldrich, Inc., St. Louis, Missouri, USA) was used as the internal standard. The FAME was separated with gas chromatograph mass spectrometer (GCMS-QP2010SE, Shimadzu, Japan). The column was SP®-2560 Capillary column (100 m × 0.25 mm × 0.20 μm, Supelco, Inc., Bellefonte, PA, USA). The operating conditions were as follows: The column oven temperature was initially held at 100 °C for 3 min, and programmed to rise at the rate of 5 °C/min to 280 °C and maintained for 20 min. Helium was the carrier gas with a flow rate of 1 mL/min (linear velocity of 44.3 cm/s and pressure of 11.604 psi) and the split ratio was 1:30. The injector temperature was set at 280 °C, while the temperature of detector (mass spectrophotometer) was maintained at 250 °C. The peaks of samples were identified, and the concentrations were calculated based on the retention time and peak areas of known standards. A reference FAME standard ((Mix C4–24, 18919-1AMP; Supelco, Bellefonte, USA) was used to determine recoveries and correction factors for the determination of individual FA.

The thrombogenicity index (TI) and Atherogenicity index (AI) of meat lipids were calculated according to Ulbricht and Southgate (1991) as follows:
TI =
C 14 : 0 + C 16 : 0 + C 18 : 0
/
0.5 × ΣMUFA + 0.5 × Σn 6 + 3 × Σn 3
+
n 3
n 6
AI =
C 12 : 0 + 4 × C 14 : 0 + C 16 : 0
/
ΣMUFA + Σn 6 + Σn 3

The sensory attributes of right LTL muscle of rabbits collected on day 1 postmortem were evaluated following the protocol described by AMSA (1995) and Meilgaard, Civille, and Carr (2007). Meat samples were placed in ziplock bags, cooked in water bath at 80 °C, for 20 min and, diced into blocks. Thereafter, each meat sample was placed in a labeled plastic plate and held at 40 °C until served. The holding time was approximately 30 min. Sixteen trained panelists (7 males and 9 females, aged 20–51 years) took part in the sensory evaluation. Senosry assessment was done with a 9–point hedonic scale (1 = “dislike extremely”, 2 = “dislike very much”, 3 = “dislike moderately”, 4 = “dislike slightly”, 5 = “neither like nor dislike”, 6 = “like slightly”, 7 = “like moderately”, 8 = “like very much”, 9 = “like extremely”) in one session. Each panelist was served three coded meat samples, which correspond to the three dietary groups. The panelists rinsed their mouth with water and unsalted crackers after evaluating each meat sample.

Normal distribution and homogeneity of variance were assessed via the Kolmogorov–Smirnov test and Levene test, respectively. The experiment was a completely randomized design with eight replicates per treatment. A pen of three rabbits was the experimental unit. Data for growth performance, carcass traits, fatty acid, and antioxidant enzymes were subjected to the generalized linear model procedure of SAS (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA). Diet was fitted as fixed effect while pen was fitted as random effect. Sensory data were analyzed in a similar manner except that panelist was included in the model, and fitted as a random effect. Level of significance was P < 0.05. Tukey HSD test was utilized to separate means. Time-dependent meat oxidative stability and physicochemical traits data were analyzed using the repeated statement of the MIXED procedure of SAS in which diet, chill storage, and their interaction were fitted as fixed effects while pen was fitted as random effect in a repeated measure analysis. The level of significance was P < 0.05. Least-square means were separated using the PDIFF option of SAS.

The growth indices of rabbits placed on diets supplemented with different levels of PCS are presented in Table 2 . Dietary inclusion of PCS lowered (P < 0.05) feed intake, and FCR and improved (P < 0.05) BWG in rabbits. The reduced FI and FCR may be attributed to the tannin content of PCS, which causes bitterness astringency. Consistently, dietary inclusion of bilberry pomace reduced FI and FCR in rabbits (Dabbou et al., 2017). The enhanced BWG and feed efficiency in the PCS-supplemented rabbits may be ascribed to the phytochemical contents of PCS, which stimulate the activities of digestive enzymes thereby promoting the efficiency of feed utilization (Makkar, Francis, & Becker, 2007). Phytochemicals exert antioxidant properties, and can thus modulate tissue and cellular redox cellular balance that can promote health and growth performance of livestock (Makkar et al., 2007). This finding aligns with the improved phenol content and antioxidant enzyme activities in the muscle of the PCS-supplemented rabbits. Moreover, phytochemicals can beneficially alter gut microflora, which could promote disease resistance, feed efficiency, and growth performance. Similar to our findings, the inclusion of dehulled white lupine seeds improved feed efficiency and BWG in rabbits (Volek et al., 2018). On the contrary, the inclusion of perilla seed (Peiretti et al., 2011) and linseed (Mattioli et al., 2020) did not affect growth performance in rabbits.

Carcass traits in rabbits supplemented with PCS are presented in Table 3 . The slaughter, hot and cold carcass weights were greater (P < 0.05) in the PCS-5 rabbits than the control rabbits. This observation was likely because of the increased BWG in the PCS-5 rabbits. Contrary to our findings, no significant improvement in carcass weight was observed following dietary inclusion of dehulled lupine seeds (Volek et al., 2018), bilberry pomace (Dabbou et al., 2017), chia seeds (Peiretti & Meineri, 2008), and linseed (Peiretti & Meineri, 2010) in rabbits. In spite of the differences in the hot and cold carcass weights between the control and PCS-5 rabbits, dress out percentage, organ weights and percentage of carcass cuts were not affected by dietary PCS. Similar results were reported in earlier studies, in which chia seed (Peiretti & Meineri, 2008), and linseed (Peiretti & Meineri, 2010) did not affect dress out percentage, organ weights and carcass cuts in rabbits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical composition, cholesterol, and total phenol content of rabbit meat
ce:section.ce:para: The inclusion of PCS did not induce significant changes (P > 0.05) on the chemical composition of the LTL muscle in rabbits (Table 4 ). This finding was likely due to the identical chemical composition of the dietary treatments. This observation agrees with those of earlier studies, in which linseed (Mattioli et al., 2020; Peiretti & Meineri, 2010), perilla seed (Peiretti et al., 2011), lupine seeds (Volek et al., 2018) and bilberry pomace (Dabbou, Gai, et al., 2017) supplementation did not alter the proximate composition of LTL in rabbits.

Muscle cholesterol reduced (P < 0.05) in response to dietary inclusion of PCS. This finding was likely due to the bioactivity of phytochemicals in PCS. Plant phytochemicals can exert inhibitory effects on the activity of 3-hydroxy-3-methyl glutaryl-CoA enzyme that is a key enzyme in cholesterol synthesis (Crowell, 1999). Further, plant phenols can hinder intestinal absorption of cholesterol or promote the excretion of bile salts (Akinleye, Kumar, Makkar, Angulo-Escalante, & Becker, 2012; Makkar et al., 2007). This observation is in tandem with those of previous reports, in which the supplementation of trub and linseed (Mattioli et al., 2020) lowered muscle cholesterol content in rabbits. The PCS supplementation improved the total phenol content in LTL muscle in rabbits. This finding could be attributed to the phytochemical content of PCS. In line with the current finding, the supplementation of 3% Lycium barbarum increased muscle phenol content in rabbits (Menchetti et al., 2020).

Dietary inclusion of PCS improved the activity of catalase and glutathione peroxidase in LTL muscle in rabbits (Table 5 ). This observation may be due to the higher concentration of n-3 PUFA in the meat of the PCS-supplemented rabbits. Increase in the activities of antioxidant enzymes in tissues may be attributed to a feedback mechanism in response to lipid oxidation (Rodriguez-Martinez & Ruiz-Torres, 1992). Our findings imply that PCS supplementation prevented the oxidative deterioration of the n-3 PUFA in LTL muscle of rabbits. Furthermore, the increase in CAT and GPx activities could be due to the antioxidant properties of the PCS phytochemicals. Phytochemicals could exert a sparing or synergistic effect on activities of antioxidant enzymes (Rajasekaran, Ravi, Sivagnanam, & Subramanian, 2006). Phytochemicals in medicinal plants could suppress or inhibit oxidation process by promoting the activity of innate antioxidant enzymes (Adeyemi, Ogundele, & Atolani, 2021; Rajasekaran et al., 2006) or by augmenting the bioavailability of ascorbic acid and tocopherol, which are potent antioxidants (Vinson, Al Kharrat, & Andreoli, 2005).

There was no significant interaction (P > 0.05) between dietary PCS and chill storage on the physicochemical properties of rabbit LTL (Table 6 ). The pH, cook loss, color coordinates, and drip loss of LTL of rabbits were unaffected (P > 0.05) by dietary inclusion of PCS. The homogenous dietary energy and husbandry conditions utilized during the trial may probably explain the uniformity in muscle pH among the diets. Livestock production factors such as quantity and quality of ration, stocking density, level of confinement, slaughter weight and age, level of stress prior to and during slaughter, can affect glycogen reserves in animals, and, therefore, postmortem lactic acid production, and, consequently muscle pH (Dalle Zotte2002; Dalle Zotte, Rémignon, & Ouhayoun, 2005; Salwani et al., 2016).

Further, the similar muscle pH among the dietary groups could likely account for the lack of differences in cook loss, color coordinates, and drip loss among the dietary treatments. The absence of differences in color coordinates among the diets may imply that dietary PCS did not alter the concentration, and oxidative status of meat pigments. These findings support those of previous report, in which the supplementation of linseed did not influence the pH, and cooking loss of rabbit meat (Bianchi, Petracci, & Cavani, 2009; Mattioli et al., 2020). Further, bilberry pomace supplementation did not affect the color and pH of LTL in rabbits (Dabbou, Gai, et al., 2017) while perilla seed supplementation did not affect the pH, color, and cook loss of rabbit meat (Peiretti et al., 2011).

Meat redness and pH reduced (P < 0.05) while the lightness, carbonyl content, drip loss, and TBARS increased (P < 0.05) during chill storage (Table 6). The decline in muscle pH during chill storage could be explained by postmortem glycolysis resulting from the conversion of glycogen to lactate. The increase in drip loss during chill storage was probably due to the rigor-induced decline in the water holding capacity of myofibrillar proteins (Adeyemi, Shittu, Sabow, Karim, & Sazili, 2017). The decline in redness and the increase in lightness during chill storage were probably due to the decline in myoglobin concentration, and/or the increase in metmyoglobin concentration.

Dietary inclusion of PCS did not affect the carbonyl and malondialdehyde contents of LTL muscle in rabbits. Owing to the higher n-3 PUFA content in the LTL of the PCS-supplemented rabbits, we expected a higher oxidative deterioration. Thus, these findings inferred that the PCS phenols protected the n-3 PUFA-enriched rabbit meat from oxidative deteriorations. Further, the increase in the catalase and glutathione peroxidase activities in the LTL of PCS-supplemented rabbits might have counteracted the oxidative deterioration in the n-3 PUFA enriched rabbit meat. Consistently, dietary billberry pomace did not alter the TBARS value of LTL in rabbits (Dabbou, Gai, et al., 2017). Contrarily, the inclusion of linseed increased carbonyl content and TBARS values of LTL in rabbits (Mattioli et al., 2020). Regardless of dietary treatment, as chill storage progressed, we observed an increase in the carbonyl and malondialdehyde contents of rabbit LTL, which indicated a decline in oxidative stability.

The FA profile of LTL muscle of rabbits supplemented with PCS is outlined in Table 7 . The total fatty acid was not influenced (P > 0.05) by dietary PCS. Previous studies did not observe significant changes in total FA of LTL of rabbits following linseed (Bianchi et al., 2009; Kouba, Benatmane, Blochet, & Mourot, 2008) and bilberry pomace (Dabbou, Gai, et al., 2017) supplementation. Significant changes (P < 0.05) were noted in the concentration of C12:0, C16:0, C18:2n-6, C18:3n-3, C20:5n-3, C22:5n-3 and C22:6n-3 in LTL muscle. Dietary PCS lowered (P < 0.05) the concentration of C12:0 and C16:0, in the LTL of rabbits. A probable explanation could be that the greater concentration of the n-3 FA in the PCS-supplemented rabbits down-regulated the activities of the lipogenic enzymes responsible for the synthesis of the medium chain fatty acids. Similarly, dietary inclusion of linseed (Bianchi et al., 2009; Kouba et al., 2008; Mattioli et al., 2020) and perilla seed (Peiretti et al., 2011) reduced C16:0 in rabbit meat while dietary inclusion of lupine seeds reduced the concentration of C12:0 in rabbit meat (Volek et al., 2018). Nonetheless, dietary lupine seeds increased the concentration of C16.0 and C14:0 in rabbit meat (Volek et al., 2018). The reduction in C16:0 and C12:0 brought about a significant reduction (P < 0.05) in the concentration of total saturated fatty acids (SFA) in the meat of the PCS-supplemented rabbits.

The concentration of individual monounsaturated fatty acids (MUFAs) was not influenced (P > 0.05) by dietary inclusion of PCS. Consequently, the total MUFA was similar (P > 0.05) among the diets. Similarly, linseed supplementation did not affect individual MUFA and total MUFA concentration in rabbit meat (Bianchi et al., 2009). In contrast, dietary inclusion of linseed (Mattioli et al., 2020) and perilla seed (Peiretti et al., 2011) lowered MUFA concentration in rabbit meat while dietary lupine seed increased MUFA concentration in rabbit meat (Volek et al., 2018 ).

The lower (P < 0.05) concentration of C18:2n-6 and the higher (P < 0.05) concentration of C18:3n-3 in the LTL of the PCS-supplemented rabbits mimicked dietary fatty acids. In agreement with these findings, the supplementation of bilberry pomace (Dabbou, Renna, et al., 2017), linseed (Mattioli et al., 2020), perilla seed (Peiretti et al., 2011) and chia seed (Peiretti & Meineri, 2008) reduced C18:2n-6 and increased C18:3n-3 concentration in rabbits. The higher (P < 0.05) concentration of C20:5n-3, C22:6n-3, and C22:5n-3 in the PCS-2.5 and PCS-5 rabbits was likely due to the increase in the concentration of C18:3n-3, because the n-3 FAs are long chain metabolites of C18:3n-3. Consequently, the LTL of PCS-2.5 and PCS-5 rabbits had higher (P < 0.05) total n-3 FA compared with that of the PCS-0 rabbits. Similarly, supplementation of linseed (Mattioli et al., 2020), bilberry pomace (Dabbou, Gai, et al., 2017), and chia seed (Peiretti & Meineri, 2008) improved the concentration of C20:5n-3, C22:6n-3, C22:5n-3, and total n-3 PUFA in LTL of rabbits.

The PUFA/SFA, and n-6/n-3 ratios, TI and AI are healthy indicators of FA in foods. In this study, the PUFA/SFA ranged between 0.87 and 1.04, and these values were beneficially higher than the recommended minimum of 0.40 (UK Department of Health, 1994; WHO, 2008). Dietary inclusion of PCS increased the PUFA/SFA of LTL muscle in rabbits. Similarly, dietary bilberry pomace (Dabbou, Gai, et al., 2017; Dabbou, Renna, et al., 2017) and perilla seed (Peiretti et al., 2011) improved the PUFA/SFA in rabbits. The recommended n-6/n-3 ratio is <4 (UK Department of Health, 1994; WHO, 2008). This target was only met in the LTL of the PCS-supplemented rabbits compared with that of the control rabbits. Supplementation with PCS led to a significant reduction (P < 0.05) in the n-6/n-3 in LTL muscle of rabbits. In tandem with our finings, significant reduction in n-6/n-3 was found in the meat of rabbits supplemented with bilberry pomace (Dabbou, Gai, et al., 2017; Dabbou, Renna, et al., 2017), perilla seed (Peiretti et al., 2011), linseed (Mattioli et al., 2020), and lupine seed (Volek et al., 2018). Consequent upon the reduction in C12:0 and C16:0 concentration and the improvement in total n-3 PUFA, the AI and TI were lower (P < 0.05) in the LTL of the PCS-supplemented rabbits compared with that of the PCS-0 rabbits. Previous studies reported a reduction in AI and TI in the meat of rabbits supplemented with linseed (Mattioli et al., 2020), perilla seed (Peiretti et al., 2011), lupine seed (Volek et al., 2018) and bilberry pomace (Dabbou, Gai, et al., 2017; Dabbou, Renna, et al., 2017). Largely, the reduction in total SFA, n-6/n-3 ratio, TI and AI indices, and the increase in PUFA/SFA ratio by PCS supplementation implied an improvement in the nutritional quality of rabbit meat.

Modifications to animal diet or production system can affect consumer acceptability of meat (Castrica et al., 2020; Dal Bosco et al., 2004; Dalle Zotte, 2002). Herein, no significant changes (P > 0.05) were observed in the sensory properties of LTL of rabbits supplemented with different levels of PCS (Table 8 ). This observation was expected owing to the similarity in the chemical composition, and total FA content of LTL across the dietary treatments. This observation is consistent with those of previous studies, in which dietary bilberry pomace (Dabbou, Gai, et al., 2017) linseed (Cullere et al., 2018) and chia seed (Meineri, Cornale, Tassone, & Peiretti, 2010) did not influence the sensorial quality of rabbit meat.

Dietary inclusion of P. conophora seed reduces cholesterol and SFA contents, and improves the n-3 PUFA content without impairing the physicochemical properties sensorial quality, and oxidative stability of LTL muscle in rabbits. The current results evince that the inclusion of P. conophora seed in the diets of rabbits could be an effective strategy in ameliorating the nutritional quality of rabbit meat. These results would be expedient in the production of better quality rabbit meat, particularly in Africa. Moreover, the 5% PCS enhances body weight gain and feed efficiency in rabbits. Further investigations to assess the impact of PCS supplementation on quality attributes of other muscles in rabbits, and the molecular mechanisms by which dietary PCS modulates muscle fatty acids are recommended.

Kazeem D. Adeyemi: Conceptualization, Project administration, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Taiwo O. Oladele: Project administration, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Methodology, Resources. Olubunmi Atolani: Investigation, Methodology, Resources. Rasheed O. Sulaimon: Investigation, Methodology, Resources. Jamiu I. Zubair: Investigation, Methodology, Resources. Babatunde H. Ajao: Investigation, Methodology, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
